title: The one skill that separates senior devs from juniors (it's not coding)
post:
After 15 years in software, I've watched hundreds of developers level up. The ones who become senior engineers fastest? They don't write more code.

They communicate better.

The ability to explain a complex technical problem to a non-technical stakeholder, to ask the right questions before writing a single line, to say 'I don't know' without ego—these are the skills that separate the seniors from the juniors.

I've seen brilliant coders stall in their careers because they couldn't articulate why their approach was better. And I've seen developers who struggled with algorithms become technical leads because they could align a team around a vision.

What do you think separates great engineers from good ones?
